file.content:
---
title: 'Week 6: Making Untidy Data Tidy'
author: "Ellen Bledsoe"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
output: pdf_document
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```

# Making Untidy Data Tidy

One major task of working with data (especially other people's data!) is to tidy up the dataset so that we can successfully calculate our descriptive statistics with `group_by` and `summarize`, make our data visualizations with `ggplot2`, and eventually conduct our statistical analyses.

This week's lessons are all about how to make untidy data into tidy data. First, let's remind ourselves what out "tidy" data means.

![](images/tidy_data.jpeg){width="75%" fig-align="center"}

Unfortunately, a lot of existing data doesn't follow these rules. The way in which datasets are untidy are always unique.

However, to analyze the data, we typically need data to be in a tidy format. We can use a number of functions from the `tidyr` package in the `tidyverse` to help make the data tidy.

## Set-up

### Packages

First, let's load the `tidyverse`.

```{r}

```

### File Paths

Now, let's read in our data. In this lesson, we are going to start incorporating a little bit of information about file paths into our work.

So far, our data files have been in the same place ("working directory" or folder) as our Quarto files. Now, however, we have our data in a data folder.

In order to tell R which data file we want to use (or where to save a data file we want to download), we have to point R to the exact location, which is no longer the same place as where our Quarto file is stored.

To do so, we must tell our to first look in the `data` folder and *then* tell it which file we want to read in.

The data file we are using is called `macroplot_data.txt`. When we open the file and take a look at it, we can see that it is tab-delimited, meaning we will want to use the `read_tsv()` function.

```{r}

```

### Data Structure

Let's take a look. These data are tree girths from the Western Ghats, a mountainous region on the western edge of the Indian peninsula. This area is considered one of the top biodiversity hotspots in the world.

Any time that a tree had more than one stem (trunk), the diameter of each stem got entered into a new column.

Is this good data structure? Why or why not? What do we need to fix?

Before we get started, let's add a `treeid` column to our data frame using the `mutate` function from `dplyr`. We want one `treeid` for each row because there is one tree for each row. We can use the `.before` argument to determine where the new column in located in the data frame.

Because the `Plot ID` column has a space in its name, we can't reference it as we normally would. Instead, we can put backticks around it.

```{r}

```

## Renaming Columns

After a while, putting backticks around column names is going to get tedious. Thankfully, the `tidyverse` has a helpful function for renaming columns.

The argument in `rename()` is the new name of the column equal to the old name.

```{r}

```

That is much better!

## Completing Data with Gaps

When recording data, people might write out a value once and then leave the following rows blank, assuming that all of the following rows are the same value until a new value is present.

This is human-readable but not computer-readable.

We can fill in these gaps using the `fill()` function. The default direction is "down," but other directions are possible.

```{r}

```

## Wide Data vs. Long Data

Data are often structured in a way that doesn't allow us to (easily) perform calculations or plot the data. We, therefore, need to wrangle data between "wide" format and "long format" to get the data in a structure that better serves our purposes.

One format isn't necessary better than the other in terms of "tidy" data, although you would often hear people use tidy data and long format data interchangeably. There are some instances where the "longest" form of the data is not actually considered tidy data.

Here, we are going to focus on converting between the types using two functions from the `tidyr` package (part of the `tidyverse`, of course!): `pivot_longer()` and `pivot_wider()`.

Like with the `join` functions, visualizing how these functions work takes a bit of practice. For GIFs demonstrating them, head over to the trusty [tidyexplain](https://www.garrickadenbuie.com/project/tidyexplain/#tidy-data) site.

**Wide Data**

In this example of wide data, the data table has one row per tree species with columns for each site.

![](images/wide_data.png){width="50%" fig-align="center"}

**Long Data**

In this example of long data, the data table has one row per tree species per site, and columns represent unique variables. In this example, the long format is considered tidy data.

![](images/long_data.png){width="50%" fig-align="center"}

One common issue with wide data is that data is spread over multiple columns that should be in one. For example, in the tables above, the "Sites" as column names are violating the rule of not having data in column names. Instead, that data should be in one column, like in the long data.

To get the data in this form, we can use a function from `tidyr` called `pivot_longer`.

## Pivot Longer

The `pivot_longer()` function takes the following arguments (as well as many other optional arguments). This function was previously called `spread()`, which you might still see in other people's code sometimes.

-   the data frame
-   columns to include (or not include)
-   `names_to`: the name of the new column to put the previous column names in
-   `values_to`: the name of the new column to put the values from the cells in

Any redundant or unnecessary columns will be automatically removed.

```{r}

```

As a reminder, the colon specifies all columns starting at `TreeGirth1` and ending at `TreeGirth5`

### Replace Values with `NA`

As you might have noticed, there are still 0s where there were no stems. We probably don't want those zeros to still be there. Instead, we might want them to be `NA` values.

To do so, we can use a function (from `dplyr`) called `na_if`. This function allows us to replace certain values with `NA`. We can use this function within a `mutate` function.

The arguments in `na_if` are the vector (column) you want the values replaced in and the value to be replaced.

```{r}

```

### Drop Rows with `NA` Values

Often, we might want to remove these rows altogether. To remove rows that have `NA` values in a specific column, we could use the `filter` function and `!is.na()`, as we have in the past.

Alternatively, we could use the `drop_na()` function, which does the same thing.

```{r}

```

### Let's Practice!

In the assignment, work on Question 1a and 1b.

### `extract()` Values from a Column

There are a number of ways that we can work with character strings, and we will cover many of those more in depth in another lesson. For now, we will stick to one helpful function called `extract()`.

Let's say we want the `Stem` column to have only the number of the stem (1-5) rather than "TreeGirth1".

`extract()` will extract one or more values from a column. It uses something called "regular expressions." We might get into details about regular expression later, but we won't worry about them too much for now.

The arguments for `extract` are:

-   the data frame
-   the name of the column from which we want to extract something (`col`)
-   the name(s) of the new column(s) (`into`)
-   the "regular expression" specifying what to extract (we will talk more about regular expressions, or "regex", later in the semester; `regex`)

```{r}

```

Here, `TreeGirth.` means the phrase "TreeGirth" followed by a single value. The `()` indicate what part of this string to extract, so just the number at the end of the string.

This gives us the result we want, with just the stem number in the `Stem` column.

You may notice that this number is on the left side of the column, not the right. That's because the number is still stored as a character, because it was extracted from a character string.

To convert it to it's actual type (numeric), we can add the optional argument `convert = TRUE` to `extract`.

```{r}

```

Adding the `convert = TRUE` argument is a helpful addition when extracting numbers so you can then work with the column as numbers.

### `separate()` a Column into Multiple Columns

In the `SpCode` column, the Genus and Species information for each tree are combined in a single column. If we want to group by genera, for example, having these data separated might be useful.

We can do so using the `separate()` function, which takes the following arguments:

We can do so using the `separate()` function, which takes the following arguments:

-   the data frame
-   the name of the column to separate (`col`)
-   new column names (`into`)
-   the separator value, character, or position (if character, it must be in quotation marks, e.g., "-"; `sep`)

```{r}

```

## Pivot Wider

Occasionally, we need to convert data the other way: from long to wide.

For example, this is fairly common for analyses of community-level data. Many packages will want cross-tab (or wide) data, such as a site by species matrix.

Let's demonstrate with an example calculating the number of stems per species. First, we need to group and summarize the data.

```{r}

```

To get the site by species matrix, we need to make the dataset wider. Since the species ID will end up as column names, we need to bring the columns back together.

### Let's Practice!

Work on Question 2 in the assignment.

### `unite` Columns into One

To bring multiple columns together into one column, we use a function that does the opposite of `separate()`: `unite()`.

The `unite()` function takes the following arguments:

-   the data frame
-   the name of the new column to hold the united date (`col`); this should be written in quotation marks
-   the columns to be united 
-   the separator value, character, or position (`sep`)

```{r}

```

### Replacing Values

There is also one species code for unknown species. While in long format we would likely convert this to an `NA` value, because it will become a column name, we probably want to make it something interpretable.

To do this, we can use a combination of the mutate function with the `replace` function. The `replace` function is part of the `base` package that comes build into R.

The `replace` function, when used inside the `mutate` function, the arguments are:

-   the name of the column
-   the condition that, if met, means the value will be replaced
-   the value to use as the replacement

```{r}

```

Now we can go ahead and convert the data into a wider format.

### `pivot_wider()`

Using `pivot_wider()` will spread values from one column into column names. It will then fill in the appropriate values. Like `pivot_longer()`, `pivot_wider()` has an older counterpart that you will likely still see called `gather()`.

`pivot_wider()` has many arguments, but these are the required ones:

-   the data frame
-   the name of column to use for column names
-   the name of column that contains the values to fill in the cells

```{r}

```

Ok, that is a lot of `NA` values...

In this case, it would likely make sense to replace these `NA` values with 0. We can do this in a couple ways. With `pivot_wider()`, there is an optional argument to specify what value to use for any empty cells after the conversion.

```{r}

```

You should now be able to complete the assignment!

     2→title: "Week 6 Assignment"

     8→```{r setup, include=FALSE}

    11→
    12→# Week 6 Assignment
    13→
    14→### Purpose
    15→
    16→The goal of this assignment is to practice converting messy data into tidy data.
    17→
    18→### Task
    19→
    20→Write R code to successfully answer each question below.
    21→
    22→### Criteria for Success
    23→
    24→-   Code is within the provided code chunks or new code chunks are created where necessary
    25→-   Code chunks run without errors
    26→-   Code chunks have brief comments indicating which code is answering which part of the question
    27→-   Code will be assessed as follows:
    28→    -   Produces the correct answer using the requested approach: 100%
    29→    -   Generally uses the right approach, but a minor mistake results in an incorrect answer: 90%
    30→    -   Attempts to solve the problem and makes some progress using the core concept, but returns the wrong answer and does not demonstrate comfort with the core concept: 50%
    31→    -   Answer demonstrates a lack of understanding of the core concept: 0%
    32→-   Any questions requiring written answers are answered with sufficient detail
    33→
    34→### Due Date
    35→
    36→Oct 7 at 2:00 pm
    37→
    38→# Assignment Exercises
    39→
    40→### Set-up
    41→
    42→Load the packages we will need. You can either load all of them individually (`readr`, `dplyr`, `tidyr`, `ggplot2`) or load the `tidyverse` package.
    43→
    44→```{r}
    45→
    46→```
    47→
    48→### 1. Forest Area per Country (15 pts)
    49→
    50→The data in `forest_per_country.csv` are downloaded from the WHO and contain the amount of forest (sq. km) per country.
    51→
    52→First, find this CSV file in the `data` folder in the "Files" tab. Click on the CSV file and select "View File." You might notice that the top of the document looks a bit odd.
    53→
    54→The first 3 rows of the file are metadata or empty, which we do not want.
    55→
    56→When you use `read_csv` to read in the data and save the output, you will want to do a few things:
    57→
    58→-   ensure you are using the correct file path (pointing R into the `data` folder first, then the name of the file)
    59→-   add the arguments `skip = 4` and `col_names = TRUE` to the `read_csv` function to deal with the rows of metadata.
    60→
    61→a.  Currently, this data is in a wide format. We want to convert this to a longer format and make it tidy. Use the `pivot_longer` function to do so. Save the output as an object.
    62→
    63→    Because the column names start with numbers, which R does not like, we need to put the column names either in backticks or quotation marks (e.g., "1990":"2022").
    64→
    65→b.  Remove any rows from the output of (a) that have `NA` in the forest area column using the `drop_na()` function.
    66→
    67→c.  Let's remind ourselves how to plot the data. Make a scatterplot of the data with year on the x-axis and forest area on the y-axis. Make the points partially transparent and the color "forestgreen." Add more descriptive axes labels and a theme.
    68→
    69→    Add the following line of code to the end of your ggplot code so we can see the years along the x-axis: `theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, vjust = 0.5))`.
    70→
    71→d.  In the "Files" tab, click on the "New Folder" button, and create a new folder called "plots". Using the `ggsave()` function and your knowledge of file paths, save the plot you created in (c) directly into the "plots" folder.
    72→
    73→### 2. OECD Data (10 pts)
    74→
    75→The `oecd_annual_data.csv` contains data from the Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD) about various global fishing economies and sustainability. This dataset has the area of protected marine reserves.
    76→
    77→a.  Like the forest data from Question 1, this data has a few rows of metadata at the top of the document that we need to skip. Use the same arguments as we did in Question 1 (same values, as well) when reading in the file.
    78→
    79→b.  Use the `fill()` function to fill in the missing values in the first column. Save the output as an object to be used in (c).
    80→
    81→c.  Using the data frame you created in (b), put the data in a tidy format. You'll need to use the same trick with the year column names as you did in 1a. Save the output as an object.
    82→
    83→d.  Using the data frame you created in (c), only keep the rows for non-OECD members. Then, wrangle the data so that it is back in wide format. This time, however, each row should represent a year and each country will have its own column. Your final data frame should only have a year column and each country's column.
    84→
    85→### 3. Santa Cruz Rodents Data Cleaning (20 pts)
    86→
    87→Start by reading in the rodent data from the Santa Cruz River, `capture_data.csv`. You won't need any additional arguments this time.
    88→
    89→Take a look at the data. You'll likely notice immediately that there are some issues to be fixed.
    90→
    91→For this question, there isn't really a good way for me to show you the output in the answer key; you'll want to take a look at the data frame in R and/or make good use of the filter function to make sure the issue got fixed.
    92→
    93→a.  Using the `rename()` function, rename any column that should to be renamed according to good column naming practices. Save the output.
    94→
    95→b.  Using the saved output from (a), we next need to fill in the missing values in the `Site` column. Save the output.
    96→
    97→c.  In the `Species` column, there are 2 different species that have question marks next to their names. Using the output from (b), use the `replace` function inside of a `mutate` function to remove the question marks (e.g., `SIOC?` should become `SIOC` and `DIME?` should become `DIME`. Again, save the output.
    98→
    99→    (This is just for practice. In reality, we might want to create a code for unknown species or a column for unclear ID).
   100→
   101→d.  If we look at the data classes for the columns, we can see that the column for tail length is character when it should be numeric. This usually indicates that there is a special character or letter somewhere in the column. As it turns out, in the last row, the value is `~15.5` instead of `15.5`. Using the output from (c), use the `replace` function inside of `mutate` to convert that value to `15.5`.
   102→

   104→
   105→f.  Let's practice plotting again! Create a series of histograms (subplots within one larger plot) of the rodents' total weights, one histogram per species. Have the color of the histograms be determined by the site. Each histogram should be partially transparent, overlapping (not stacked), and the width of the bins should be 10. Finally, edit the axes labels and apply a theme. Save the output into the "plots" folder using file paths.
   106→
   107→### 4. Remembering Joins (15 pts)
   108→
   109→Let's remind ourselves about joins from Week 4.
   110→
   111→Read in the vegetation data that goes along with the Santa Cruz rodent data. The .csv file is called `microsite_grouped_veg.csv`. We will be joining this dataframe with the rodent dataframe from Question 3.
   112→
   113→a.  Using the `rename` function rename any columns that should be renamed, using best practices in naming. Use a consistent structure (and make the `Site` and `Trap Location` column names match those from the rodents data frame in Question 3). Save the output.
   114→
   115→b.  Using the output from (a), select the `Site`, `Trap Location` and `Grouped Veg` columns and save those as a new data frame. Save the output.
   116→
   117→c.  Using the output from (b) and the final rodent data frame from Question 3, use an `inner_join()` to join those two data frames.
   118→
   119→d.  In your own words (\~2-3 sentences), explain how the inner join in (c) worked to combine the data in these two dataframes.
   120→
   121→### 5. Santa Cruz Rodents Wrangling (20 pts)
   122→
   123→Let's practice splitting and combining columns as well as pivoting the Santa Cruz rodent data (from Question 3).
   124→
   125→a.  Use the `separate()` function to split the date column into three separate columns. Save your output.
   126→
   127→b.  Using the output from (a), rejoin the three date columns back together into one column.
   128→

   130→

   132→
   133→### 6. Mammals (20 pts)
   134→
   135→The code chunk below has some made-up mammal data. Run the code chunk below to complete Question 6.
   136→
   137→```{r}
   138→mammals <- data.frame(site = c(1,1,2,3,3,3), 
   139→                      taxon = c('Suncus etruscus', 'Sorex cinereus', 
   140→                                'Myotis nigricans', 'Notiosorex crawfordi',

---
title: "Week 6 Assignment"
author: "Ellen Bledsoe"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
output: pdf_document
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```

# Week 6 Assignment

### Purpose

The goal of this assignment is to practice converting messy data into tidy data.

### Task

Write R code to successfully answer each question below.

### Criteria for Success

-   Code is within the provided code chunks or new code chunks are created where necessary
-   Code chunks run without errors
-   Code chunks have brief comments indicating which code is answering which part of the question
-   Code will be assessed as follows:
    -   Produces the correct answer using the requested approach: 100%
    -   Generally uses the right approach, but a minor mistake results in an incorrect answer: 90%
    -   Attempts to solve the problem and makes some progress using the core concept, but returns the wrong answer and does not demonstrate comfort with the core concept: 50%
    -   Answer demonstrates a lack of understanding of the core concept: 0%
-   Any questions requiring written answers are answered with sufficient detail

### Due Date

Oct 7 at 2:00 pm

# Assignment Exercises

### Set-up

Load the packages we will need. You can either load all of them individually (`readr`, `dplyr`, `tidyr`, `ggplot2`) or load the `tidyverse` package.

```{r}

```

### 1. Forest Area per Country (15 pts)

The data in `forest_per_country.csv` are downloaded from the WHO and contain the amount of forest (sq. km) per country.

First, find this CSV file in the `data` folder in the "Files" tab. Click on the CSV file and select "View File." You might notice that the top of the document looks a bit odd.

The first 3 rows of the file are metadata or empty, which we do not want.

When you use `read_csv` to read in the data and save the output, you will want to do a few things:

-   ensure you are using the correct file path (pointing R into the `data` folder first, then the name of the file)
-   add the arguments `skip = 4` and `col_names = TRUE` to the `read_csv` function to deal with the rows of metadata.

a.  Currently, this data is in a wide format. We want to convert this to a longer format and make it tidy. Use the `pivot_longer` function to do so. Save the output as an object.

    Because the column names start with numbers, which R does not like, we need to put the column names either in backticks or quotation marks (e.g., "1990":"2022").

b.  Remove any rows from the output of (a) that have `NA` in the forest area column using the `drop_na()` function.

c.  Let's remind ourselves how to plot the data. Make a scatterplot of the data with year on the x-axis and forest area on the y-axis. Make the points partially transparent and the color "forestgreen." Add more descriptive axes labels and a theme.

    Add the following line of code to the end of your ggplot code so we can see the years along the x-axis: `theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, vjust = 0.5))`.

d.  In the "Files" tab, click on the "New Folder" button, and create a new folder called "plots". Using the `ggsave()` function and your knowledge of file paths, save the plot you created in (c) directly into the "plots" folder.

### 2. OECD Data (10 pts)

The `oecd_annual_data.csv` contains data from the Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD) about various global fishing economies and sustainability. This dataset has the area of protected marine reserves.

a.  Like the forest data from Question 1, this data has a few rows of metadata at the top of the document that we need to skip. Use the same arguments as we did in Question 1 (same values, as well) when reading in the file.

b.  Use the `fill()` function to fill in the missing values in the first column. Save the output as an object to be used in (c).

c.  Using the data frame you created in (b), put the data in a tidy format. You'll need to use the same trick with the year column names as you did in 1a. Save the output as an object.

d.  Using the data frame you created in (c), only keep the rows for non-OECD members. Then, wrangle the data so that it is back in wide format. This time, however, each row should represent a year and each country will have its own column. Your final data frame should only have a year column and each country's column.

### 3. Santa Cruz Rodents Data Cleaning (20 pts)

Start by reading in the rodent data from the Santa Cruz River, `capture_data.csv`. You won't need any additional arguments this time.

Take a look at the data. You'll likely notice immediately that there are some issues to be fixed.

For this question, there isn't really a good way for me to show you the output in the answer key; you'll want to take a look at the data frame in R and/or make good use of the filter function to make sure the issue got fixed.

a.  Using the `rename()` function, rename any column that should to be renamed according to good column naming practices. Save the output.

b.  Using the saved output from (a), we next need to fill in the missing values in the `Site` column. Save the output.

c.  In the `Species` column, there are 2 different species that have question marks next to their names. Using the output from (b), use the `replace` function inside of a `mutate` function to remove the question marks (e.g., `SIOC?` should become `SIOC` and `DIME?` should become `DIME`. Again, save the output.

    (This is just for practice. In reality, we might want to create a code for unknown species or a column for unclear ID).

d.  If we look at the data classes for the columns, we can see that the column for tail length is character when it should be numeric. This usually indicates that there is a special character or letter somewhere in the column. As it turns out, in the last row, the value is `~15.5` instead of `15.5`. Using the output from (c), use the `replace` function inside of `mutate` to convert that value to `15.5`.

e.  In both the "Hair Sample" and "Position" columns, there is a `?`. Using the output from (d), use the `na_if` function inside a `mutate` function to convert those `?` to `NA` values.

f.  Let's practice plotting again! Create a series of histograms (subplots within one larger plot) of the rodents' total weights, one histogram per species. Have the color of the histograms be determined by the site. Each histogram should be partially transparent, overlapping (not stacked), and the width of the bins should be 10. Finally, edit the axes labels and apply a theme. Save the output into the "plots" folder using file paths.

### 4. Remembering Joins (15 pts)

Let's remind ourselves about joins from Week 4.

Read in the vegetation data that goes along with the Santa Cruz rodent data. The .csv file is called `microsite_grouped_veg.csv`. We will be joining this dataframe with the rodent dataframe from Question 3.

a.  Using the `rename` function rename any columns that should be renamed, using best practices in naming. Use a consistent structure (and make the `Site` and `Trap Location` column names match those from the rodents data frame in Question 3). Save the output.

b.  Using the output from (a), select the `Site`, `Trap Location` and `Grouped Veg` columns and save those as a new data frame. Save the output.

c.  Using the output from (b) and the final rodent data frame from Question 3, use an `inner_join()` to join those two data frames.

d.  In your own words (\~2-3 sentences), explain how the inner join in (c) worked to combine the data in these two dataframes.

### 5. Santa Cruz Rodents Wrangling (20 pts)

Let's practice splitting and combining columns as well as pivoting the Santa Cruz rodent data (from Question 3).

a.  Use the `separate()` function to split the date column into three separate columns. Save your output.

b.  Using the output from (a), rejoin the three date columns back together into one column.

c.  Summarize the data so that we have a count of each species per site. Save the output.

d.  Convert the data from (c) from long format to wide format. Use an argument in the `pivot_wider` function to have all blank cells filled with 0 instead of `NA`.

### 6. Mammals (20 pts)

The code chunk below has some made-up mammal data. Run the code chunk below to complete Question 6.

```{r}
mammals <- data.frame(site = c(1,1,2,3,3,3), 
                      taxon = c('Suncus etruscus', 'Sorex cinereus', 
                                'Myotis nigricans', 'Notiosorex crawfordi', 
                                'Suncus etruscus', 'Myotis nigricans'),
                      density = c(6.2, 5.2, 11.0, 1.2, 9.4, 9.6), # number of individuals / area
                      avg_mass = c(4.2, 5, 9.1, 8.6, 4.1, 8.7))
```

Like with many of the questions before, save your output from each task to use in the next task. You can overwrite the `mammals` object each time if you would like to.

a.  Use the `separte()` function to create columns for the genus and species (from the taxon column).

b.  Use `pivot_longer` so that `density` and `mass` end up in one column and the values end up in another column.

c.  Even though the data from (b) is longer, it isn't tidier. Explain why not.

d.  Use the `unite()` function to bring the genus and species column back together as one column with whatever separator you choose.

e.  Use `pivot_wider()` to bring the data frame back to it's original state.

file.content:
---
title: "Week 6 Assignment"
author: "Ellen Bledsoe"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
output: pdf_document
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE)
```

# Week 6 Assignment

# Assignment Exercises

### Set-up

Load the packages we will need. You can either load all of them individually (`readr`, `dplyr`, `tidyr`, `ggplot2`) or load the `tidyverse` package.

```{r message=FALSE}
library(tidyverse)
```

### 1. Forest Area per Country (15 pts)


```{r message=FALSE}
forest <- read_csv("forest_per_country.csv", skip = 4, col_names = TRUE)
```


```{r}
# a
forest <- forest %>% 
  pivot_longer(`1990`:`2022`, names_to = "Year", values_to = "ForestArea_sqkm")
forest

# b.
forest <- forest %>% 
  drop_na(ForestArea_sqkm)
forest
  
# c
ggplot(forest, aes(Year, ForestArea_sqkm)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5, color = "forestgreen") +
  labs(x = "Year",
       y = "Forest Area (sq. km)") +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, vjust = 0.5))
```

### 2. OECD Data (10 pts)


```{r message=FALSE}
print("2a")
oecd <- read_csv("oecd_annual_data.csv", skip = 4, col_names = TRUE)
oecd
```

```{r}
print("2b: last 6 rows")
oecd <- oecd %>% 
  fill(OECD_member)
tail(oecd)

print("2c")
oecd <- oecd %>% 
  pivot_longer(`2000`:`2022`, names_to = "Year", values_to = "MarineProtectedArea_sqkm")
oecd

print("2d")
oecd |> 
  filter(OECD_member == "Non-OECD Economies") |> 
  pivot_wider(names_from = Country, values_from = MarineProtectedArea_sqkm) |> 
  select(-OECD_member)
```

### 3. Santa Cruz Rodents Data Cleaning (20 pts)


```{r message=FALSE}
rodents <- read_csv("capture_data.csv")

#a
rodents <- rodents %>% 
  rename(TrapID = `Trap ID`, Status = `Status (R/N)`, TotalWeight = `Total Weight`,
         BagWeight = `Bag weight`, AnimalWeight = `Animal Weight`, 
         HindfoodLength = `Hind foot length`, TailLength = `Tail length`,
         HairSample = `Hair sample (Y/N)`, Position = `Position (R/L)`)

#b
print("3b")
rodents <- rodents %>% 
  fill(Site)
rodents

#c
rodents <- rodents %>% 
  mutate(Species = replace(Species, Species == "SIOC?", "SIOC"),
         Species = replace(Species, Species == "DIME?", "DIME"))

#d
rodents <- rodents %>% 
  mutate(TailLength = replace(TailLength, TailLength == "~15.5", "15.5"))

#e
rodents <- rodents %>% 
  mutate(HairSample = na_if(HairSample, "?"),
         Position = na_if(Position, "?"))

#f
print("3f")
ggplot(rodents, aes(TotalWeight, fill = Site)) +
  geom_histogram(alpha = 0.5, position = "identity", binwidth = 10) +
  facet_wrap(~ Species) +
  labs(x = "Weight (g)", y = "Frequency") +
  theme_classic()

```


### 4. Remembering Joins (15 pts)

```{r message=FALSE}
veg <- read_csv("microsite_grouped_veg.csv")

#a
veg <- veg %>% 
  rename(RecordID = `...1`, TrapID = `Trap Location`, DistancetoVeg_m = `Distance to Vegetation (m)`,
         VegetationType = `Type of Vegetation`, PercentCover = `Percent Veg Cover`, DistancetoWater_m = `Distance to Water (m)`)

#b
print("4b")
veg <- veg %>% 
  select(Site, TrapID, Grouped_Veg)
veg

#c
print("4c")
inner_join(rodents, veg)
```

### 5. Santa Cruz Rodents Wrangling (20 pts)

```{r}
#a
print("5a")
rodents <- rodents %>% 
  separate(Date, into = c("Year", "Month", "Day"), sep = "-")
rodents

#b
print("5b")
rodents <- rodents %>% 
  unite("Date", Year:Day, sep = "-")
rodents

#c
print("5c")
sp_by_site <- rodents %>% 
  group_by(Site, Species) %>% 
  summarize(Count = n())
sp_by_site

#d
print("5d")
sp_by_site %>% 
  pivot_wider(names_from = Species,
              values_from = Count,
              values_fill = 0)
```

### 6. Mammals (20 pts)

The code chunk below has some made-up mammal data. Run the code chunk below to complete question 5.

```{r}
mammals <- data.frame(site = c(1,1,2,3,3,3), 
                      taxon = c('Suncus etruscus', 'Sorex cinereus', 
                                'Myotis nigricans', 'Notiosorex crawfordi', 
                                'Suncus etruscus', 'Myotis nigricans'),
                      density = c(6.2, 5.2, 11.0, 1.2, 9.4, 9.6),
                      avg_mass = c(4.2, 5, 9.1, 8.6, 4.1, 8.7))
```

```{r}
print("6a")
mammals <- mammals %>% 
  separate(taxon, c("genus", "species"), sep = " ")
mammals

print("6b")
mammals <- mammals %>% 
  pivot_longer(density:avg_mass, names_to = "measurement", values_to = "value")
mammals

print("6c")
mammals <- mammals %>% 
  unite("taxon", genus, species, sep = " ")
mammals

print("6d")
mammals <- mammals %>% 
  pivot_wider(names_from = measurement, values_from = value)
mammals
```

Add the data file (checkbox) 86→2. Commit it 87→ 88→- Git -\> Select `Gaeta_etal_CLC_data.csv`. 89→- Commit with message. 90→ - `Add fish size and growth rate data` 91→- History: 92→ - One commit 93→ - Changes too large to see 94→ 95→#### Commit R script 96→ 97→- Read in data to new R script. 98→ 99→```{r} 100→library(tidyverse) 101→ 102→fish_data <- read_csv("Gaeta_etal_CLC_data.csv") 103→``` 104→ 105→- Save as `fish-analysis.qmd`. 106→- Git -\> Select `fish-analysis.qmd`. 107→ - Changes in staged files will be included in next commit. 108→ - Can also see changes by selecting `Diff` 109→- Commit with message. 110→ - `Start script comparing fish length and scale size` 111→- History: 112→ - Two commits 113→ - See what changes were made to `fish-analysis.qmd` 114→ 115→### Building a history 116→ 117→- `fish-analysis.qmd` doesn't currently show on the `Git` tab 118→ - No saved changes since last commit 119→- Add some more code to `fish-analysis.R` 120→ - Create new categorical size column 121→ 122→```{r} 123→fish_data_cat = fish_data |> 124→ mutate(length_cat = if_else(length > 200, "big", "small")) 125→``` 126→ 127→- Save `fish-analysis.qmd`. 128→- Now we see the file on the `Git` tab. 129→ - `M` indicates that it's been modified. 130→- To commit these changes, we need to stage the file. 131→ - Check the box next to `fish-analysis.R`. 132→- Commit with message. 133→ - `Add categorical fish length column` 134→- History: 135→ - Three commits 136→ - Each `fish-analysis.R` commit shows the additions we made in that commit. 137→- Modify this code in `fish-analysis.R` 138→ - Change category cut-off size 139→ 140→```{r} 141→fish_data_cat = fish_data %>% 142→ mutate(length_cat = if_else(length > 300, "big", "small")) 143→``` 144→ 145→- Save file -\> stage -\> commit 146→ - `Change size cutoff for new column` 147→ - Green sections for added lines, red for deleted 148→ - Git works line by line. 149→ - The previous version of the line is shown as deleted. 150→ - The new version of the line is shown as added. 151→ 152→### Committing multiple files 153→ 154→- Commits can include multiple files at once 155→- Let's move our data file into a `data` subdirectory 156→- `New Folder` -\> `data` 157→- Checkbox `Gaeta_etal_CLC_data.csv` -\> `More` -\> `Move` 158→- Change code to read from new subdirectory 159→ 160→```{r} 161→fish_data = read.csv("data/Gaeta_etal_CLC_data.csv") 162→``` 163→ 164→- Changes to R script indicated by M 165→ 166→- Original datafile has a red D next to it which indicates "deleted" 167→ 168→- New, untracked, data directory 169→ 170→- git initially thinks we've deleted `Gaeta_etal_CLC_data.csv` and created a new `Gaeta_etal_CLC_data.csv` file in a new directory. 171→ 172→- Click on both the old and new files to stage them 173→ 174→- git then recognizes that we have moved (or renamed) the file by making the two files into one and marking this with an `R` for "rename". 175→ 176→- Commit: `Move data file into subdirectory` 177→ 178→### Git as a time machine 179→ 180→#### Experiment with impunity 181→ 182→```{r} 183→fish_data_cat = fish_data %>% 184→ mutate(length_cat = ifelse(length > 300, "large", "small")) 185→``` 186→ 187→- `Save` and show changes are staged 188→ 189→- `More` -\> `Revert` -\> `Yes` 190→ 191→- Get previous state of a file 192→ 193→ - `History` -\> select commit -\> `View file @ ...` 194→ - Save file over current file 195→ - Copy and paste relevant piece into current file 196→ 197→#### Delete with impunity 198→ 199→- Both of these also work for deleted files 200→- Close the upper left window with the `fish-analysis.R`. 201→- Choose the `File` tab in the lower right window. 202→- Select `fish-analysis.R` -\> `Delete` -\> `Yes` 203→- Stage deleted file -\> `More` -\> `Revert` -\> `Yes` 204→ 205→## GitHub Remotes 206→ 207→- So far we've worked with a local `Git` repository. 208→- One of the big benefits of version control is easy collaboration. 209→- To do this, we synchronize our local changes with a remote repository called `origin`. 210→- Our remote repository is on GitHub. 211→ - By far the most popular hosted version control site 212→ - Public and private hosted repositories 213→ - Private free for students and academics 214→ - 215→ - For the assignment, we're using private repositories that we made at the beginning. 216→ 217→### Push to a remote 218→ 219→#### Connect to GitHub 220→ 221→- To push to your remote we first have to connect to GitHub, which is a little tricky 222→- First, log in to GitHub in your browser 223→- Then create a GitHub token, this is like a special password just for one computer 224→ 225→```{r} 226→usethis::create_github_token() 227→``` 228→ 229→- Select defaults 230→ 231→- Create token 232→ 233→- Copy token 234→ 235→- Now add this token our local git setup so that it can use it to connect to GitHub 236→ 237→```{r} 238→gitcreds::gitcreds_set() 239→``` 240→ 241→- Paste your password 242→ 243→#### Push 244→ 245→- `Push` sends your recent commits to the `origin` remote. 246→- Before a `Push` your commits show in your local history but not on the remote. 247→- To `Push` to your remote, select the `Push` button at the top of the `Git` tab. 248→- Now your changes and commit history are also stored on the remote. 249→ 250→```{r} 251→ggplot(fish_data_cat, aes(x = scalelength, fill = length_cat)) + 252→ geom_histogram() 253→``` 254→ 255→> Either you (logged in as another user) or your teaching partner should make the same change to your respository 256→ 257→### Pulling 258→ 259→- Big advantage to remotes is easy collaboration 260→ 261→- Avoids emailing files and shared folders where you are never sure if you actually have the most recent version 262→ 263→- Makes it easy to see what collaborators have done 264→ 265→- Automatically combines non-overlapping changes 266→ 267→- While I've been talking, a collaborator has added a plot of scale size and fish length to the code. 268→ 269→- `Pull` the changes from the remote repo with the `Pull` button on the Git tab 270→ 271→### Merges 272→ 273→> Demo merges either with a partner or by logging into GitHub as another user in the browser. 274→ 275→- What happens if two people make changes at the same time? 276→ 277→ - If they edit different parts of the code git will combine them automatically 278→ - If they edit the same areas of the code this requires human intervention 279→ 280→- Merges 281→ 282→- You decide to change the number of histogram bins to 10 283→ 284→```{r} 285→geom_histogram(bins = 10) 286→``` 287→ 288→- Your collaborator reassesses the measurement device and decides it is accurate down to 0.5 mm and pushes the change to the remote repository [make this change in the remote] 289→ 290→```{r} 291→filter(scalelength >= 0.5) 292→``` 293→ 294→- You try to push your change 295→- Get an error that shows someone else has made a change & you need to incorporate it to push 296→- Pull 297→- Merge happens automatically 298→- You have both sets of changes 299→- Remote still only has collaborators changes 300→- Push to add the merged version to the remote 301→ 302→### Merge conflicts 303→ 304→- If both you and your collaborator edit the same location in the code git doesn't know how to combine the changes. 305→ 306→- A human has to make this kind of decision. 307→ 308→- You decide to change `"big"` to `"large"` 309→ 310→```{r} 311→mutate(length_cat = ifelse(length > 300, "large", "small")) 312→``` 313→ 314→- Your collaborator changes the size threshold and pushes to the remote 315→ 316→```{r} 317→mutate(length_cat = ifelse(length > 250, "big", "small")) 318→``` 319→ 320→- You attempt to push your changes 321→- Merge conflict when pulling collaborators changes 322→- This shows as `U` for "unmerged" in RStudio 323→- First block of code is your version 324→- Second block is the version on the remote 325→- Combine into a single block that includes everything 326→ 327→```{r} 328→mutate(length_cat = ifelse(length > 250, "large", "small")) 329→``` 330→ 331→- Click check box next to file 332→- Commit indicating that it is a merge 333→- Still not on remote yet 334→- Push 335→ Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You CAN and SHOULD provide analysis of malware, what it is doing. But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.

file.content: --- title: 'Week 9: Version Control' author: "Ellen Bledsoe" date: "`r Sys.Date()`" output: pdf_document --- ```{r setup, include=FALSE} knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE) ``` # Reproducibility ## Version Control ### git and GitHub ### Using git and GitHub in RStudio ## Introduction ### Motivation Get rid of messy folders and track changes to things like data files and code in a more manageable way. ### Benefits of version control - Track changes (*but better*) - Tracks every change ever made in groups called commits - Every commit stores the full state of all of your files at that time - Never lose anything - Revert or restore to any commit - Easily unbreak your code/data/manuscript - No more file name changes - Collaboration - Work on things simultaneously - See what changes others have made - Everyone has the most recent version of everything ## Version control using Git & RStudio ### Create a Git repo 1. Navigate to Github in a web browser and login. 2. Click the `+` at the upper right corner of the page and choose `New repository`. 3. Choose the class organization (e.g., `dcsemester`) as the `Owner` of the repo. 4. Fill in a `Repository name` that follows the form `FirstnameLastname`. 5. Select `Private`. 6. Select `Initialize this repository with a README`. 7. Click `Create Repository`. ### Connect to the Git repo in RStudio 1. From new GitHub repository, click green `Clone or download` button -\> Click the `Copy to clipboard` button. 2. In RStudio, File -\> New Project -\> Version Control -\> Git 3. Paste copied URL in `Repository URL:`. 4. Leave `Project directory name:` blank; automatically given repo name. 5. Choose where to `Create project as subdirectory of:`. 6. Click `Create Project`. 7. Check to make sure you have a `Git` tab in the upper right window. ### Install ```{r} #install.packages("usethis") library(usethis) ``` ### Introduce yourself to Git ```{r} library(usethis) use_git_config(user.name = "[name]", user.email = "[email]") ``` ### First commits #### Commit data - Download the data file [Gaeta_etal_CLC_data.csv](%7B%7B%20site.baseurl%20%7D%7D/data/Gaeta_etal_CLC_data.csv) to your project directory. - Add the data file to version control - Two step process: 1. Add the data file (checkbox) 2. Commit it - Git -\> Select `Gaeta_etal_CLC_data.csv`. - Commit with message. - `Add fish size and growth rate data` - History: - One commit - Changes too large to see #### Commit R script - Read in data to new R script. ```{r} library(tidyverse) fish_data <- read_csv("Gaeta_etal_CLC_data.csv") ``` - Save as `fish-analysis.qmd`. - Git -\> Select `fish-analysis.qmd`. - Changes in staged files will be included in next commit. - Can also see changes by selecting `Diff` - Commit with message. - `Start script comparing fish length and scale size` - History: - Two commits - See what changes were made to `fish-analysis.qmd` ### Building a history - `fish-analysis.qmd` doesn't currently show on the `Git` tab - No saved changes since last commit - Add some more code to `fish-analysis.R` - Create new categorical size column ```{r} fish_data_cat = fish_data |> mutate(length_cat = if_else(length > 200, "big", "small")) ``` - Save `fish-analysis.qmd`. - Now we see the file on the `Git` tab. - `M` indicates that it's been modified. - To commit these changes, we need to stage the file. - Check the box next to `fish-analysis.R`. - Commit with message. - `Add categorical fish length column` - History: - Three commits - Each `fish-analysis.R` commit shows the additions we made in that commit. - Modify this code in `fish-analysis.R` - Change category cut-off size ```{r} fish_data_cat = fish_data %>% mutate(length_cat = if_else(length > 300, "big", "small")) ``` - Save file -\> stage -\> commit - `Change size cutoff for new column` - Green sections for added lines, red for deleted - Git works line by line. - The previous version of the line is shown as deleted. - The new version of the line is shown as added. ### Committing multiple files - Commits can include multiple files at once - Let's move our data file into a `data` subdirectory - `New Folder` -\> `data` - Checkbox `Gaeta_etal_CLC_data.csv` -\> `More` -\> `Move` - Change code to read from new subdirectory ```{r} fish_data = read.csv("data/Gaeta_etal_CLC_data.csv") ``` - Changes to R script indicated by M - Original datafile has a red D next to it which indicates "deleted" - New, untracked, data directory - git initially thinks we've deleted `Gaeta_etal_CLC_data.csv` and created a new `Gaeta_etal_CLC_data.csv` file in a new directory. - Click on both the old and new files to stage them - git then recognizes that we have moved (or renamed) the file by making the two files into one and marking this with an `R` for "rename". - Commit: `Move data file into subdirectory` ### Git as a time machine #### Experiment with impunity ```{r} fish_data_cat = fish_data %>% mutate(length_cat = ifelse(length > 300, "large", "small")) ``` - `Save` and show changes are staged - `More` -\> `Revert` -\> `Yes` - Get previous state of a file - `History` -\> select commit -\> `View file @ ...` - Save file over current file - Copy and paste relevant piece into current file #### Delete with impunity - Both of these also work for deleted files - Close the upper left window with the `fish-analysis.R`. - Choose the `File` tab in the lower right window. - Select `fish-analysis.R` -\> `Delete` -\> `Yes` - Stage deleted file -\> `More` -\> `Revert` -\> `Yes` ## GitHub Remotes - So far we've worked with a local `Git` repository. - One of the big benefits of version control is easy collaboration. - To do this, we synchronize our local changes with a remote repository called `origin`. - Our remote repository is on GitHub. - By far the most popular hosted version control site - Public and private hosted repositories - Private free for students and academics - - For the assignment, we're using private repositories that we made at the beginning. ### Push to a remote #### Connect to GitHub - To push to your remote we first have to connect to GitHub, which is a little tricky - First, log in to GitHub in your browser - Then create a GitHub token, this is like a special password just for one computer ```{r} usethis::create_github_token() ``` - Select defaults - Create token - Copy token - Now add this token our local git setup so that it can use it to connect to GitHub ```{r} gitcreds::gitcreds_set() ``` - Paste your password #### Push - `Push` sends your recent commits to the `origin` remote. - Before a `Push` your commits show in your local history but not on the remote. - To `Push` to your remote, select the `Push` button at the top of the `Git` tab. - Now your changes and commit history are also stored on the remote. ```{r} ggplot(fish_data_cat, aes(x = scalelength, fill = length_cat)) + geom_histogram() ``` > Either you (logged in as another user) or your teaching partner should make the same change to your respository ### Pulling - Big advantage to remotes is easy collaboration - Avoids emailing files and shared folders where you are never sure if you actually have the most recent version - Makes it easy to see what collaborators have done - Automatically combines non-overlapping changes - While I've been talking, a collaborator has added a plot of scale size and fish length to the code. - `Pull` the changes from the remote repo with the `Pull` button on the Git tab ### Merges > Demo merges either with a partner or by logging into GitHub as another user in the browser. - What happens if two people make changes at the same time? - If they edit different parts of the code git will combine them automatically - If they edit the same areas of the code this requires human intervention - Merges - You decide to change the number of histogram bins to 10 ```{r} geom_histogram(bins = 10) ``` - Your collaborator reassesses the measurement device and decides it is accurate down to 0.5 mm and pushes the change to the remote repository [make this change in the remote] ```{r} filter(scalelength >= 0.5) ``` - You try to push your change - Get an error that shows someone else has made a change & you need to incorporate it to push - Pull - Merge happens automatically - You have both sets of changes - Remote still only has collaborators changes - Push to add the merged version to the remote ### Merge conflicts - If both you and your collaborator edit the same location in the code git doesn't know how to combine the changes. - A human has to make this kind of decision. - You decide to change `"big"` to `"large"` ```{r} mutate(length_cat = ifelse(length > 300, "large", "small")) ``` - Your collaborator changes the size threshold and pushes to the remote ```{r} mutate(length_cat = ifelse(length > 250, "big", "small")) ``` - You attempt to push your changes - Merge conflict when pulling collaborators changes - This shows as `U` for "unmerged" in RStudio - First block of code is your version - Second block is the version on the remote - Combine into a single block that includes everything ```{r} mutate(length_cat = ifelse(length > 250, "large", "small")) ``` - Click check box next to file - Commit indicating that it is a merge - Still not on remote yet - Push

content.content:
1→---
     2→title: 'Week 12: Geospatial Data'
     3→author: "Jeff Oliver"
     4→date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
     5→output: pdf_document
     6→---
     7→
     8→```{r setup, include=FALSE}
     9→knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
    10→```
    11→
    12→# Geospatial Data
    13→
    14→## Introduction
    15→
    16→Geospatial data are data that have some connection to specific places or areas 
    17→on Earth. In this lesson we will look at common geospatial data types and how 
    18→we can work with them in R. We will really be scratching the surface today and 
    19→you can find links to more resources at the end of the lesson.
    20→
    21→### Setup
    22→
    23→To work with geospatial data in R, we usually need to install one or more 
    24→additional packages. The two most popular packages for geospatial data are 
    25→[terra](https://rspatial.org/pkg/) and [sf](https://r-spatial.github.io/sf/). 
    26→In this lesson, we will only be working with terra, and you can find links for 
    27→more information on terra and on sf at the bottom of the lesson. In order to 
    28→use the terra package, we must first install the package with 
    29→`install.packages()`:
    30→
    31→```{r terra-install}
    32→
    33→```
    34→
    35→And we load it into memory with the `library()` command:
    36→
    37→```{r terra-load-print}
    38→
    39→```
    40→
    41→### Some Terms
    42→
    43→Geospatial data generally come in one of two types: raster data or vector data. 
    44→
    45→+ **Raster** data are gridded data. They generally look like images and have a 
    46→fixed resolution. This means that as you zoom in on raster data, you eventually 
    47→start seeing the grids. Like when you zoom in on a photograph and it becomes 
    48→"pixelated" - we will see an example of this later. Raster data are usually 
    49→quantitative measurements of some sort, such as temperature, precipitation, and
    50→reflectance in certain light wavelengths.
    51→+ **Vector**, or shape, data are defined by specific coordinates on the Earth's 
    52→surface and do not, technically, have a fixed resolution. This means that as 
    53→you zoom in on Vector data, it never becomes "pixelated". Vector data usually 
    54→take the form of points (a single pair of coordinates), lines (two or more 
    55→coordinate pairs connected in a sequence), or polygons (three or more 
    56→coordinate pairs connected in a closed sequence). Note: I apologize at this 
    57→point for any confusion between the definition of geospatial vector data and 
    58→the vector data structure in R. They are not the same thing, even though they 
    59→probably have similar roots.
    60→
    61→![Raster data have a fixed resolution and become "pixelated" when magnifyings](zoom-raster.png)
    62→
    63→![Vector data do not lose resolution when magnifying](zoom-vector.png)
    64→
    65→
    66→### Today's Destination
    67→
    68→Today we will work with both raster and vector data, loading it into R, doing
    69→some exploratory visualization, and modifying the data. Ultimately, we are 
    70→going to create a map of temperature data of southwestern North America, 
    71→highlighting those areas that are desert biomes. At the end, we should have a 
    72→map that looks like:
    73→
    74→![Mean annual temperature of southwestern North America](sw-desert-heat.png)
    75→
    76→## Geospatial Raster Data
    77→
    78→### Reading and Visualizing Raster Data
    79→
    80→To read raster data into R, we use the `rast()` function from the terra 
    81→library; if you see an error that says something like 
    82→`Error in rast("global_temperature.tif") : could not find function "rast"`, it 
    83→probably means you need to load the terra library with `library(terra)`.
    84→
    85→```{r read-temperature-data}
    86→
    87→```
    88→
    89→With raster data, they will all (ideally) contain metadata - we can see those 
    90→metadata by running a line of code with the name of the variable that that we 
    91→read the raster into:
    92→
    93→```{r print-temperature-data}
    94→
    95→```
    96→
    97→So there is a lot packed into the output. For our reality check, we will just 
    98→note that the data are global (see the values in the "extent" field) and the 
    99→range of values for mean annual temperature seem right (see the "min value" and 
   100→"max value" fields).
   101→
   102→But come on! We are here for maps. Let us see a map! We can plot the actual 
   103→data stored in the raster with the `plot()` command.
   104→
   105→```{r plot-global-temperature-data}
   106→
   107→```
   108→
   109→### Aside: `plot()` actually means a lot of different things.
   110→
   111→A quick note that when we call `plot()`, R will take a look at what information 
   112→we passed to the function, then decide which _version_ of the `plot()` function
   113→to use. We will not go into additional detail here, but consider looking at the 
   114→documentation for the base R version of `plot()` (via `?plot`) and comparing 
   115→that with the version that comes with the terra package (via `?terra::plot`).
   116→
   117→### Cropping data
   118→
   119→Rarely do we need a map of the entire globe. Rather, we often want to focus on 
   120→parts of the globe. In this lesson we are going to focus on southwestern North 
   121→America, so we need to crop our raster data to that region. In order to do this 
   122→we first define what is called a geographic extent. This is basically a fancy 
   123→way of drawing a line around the area we are interested in. In our case, we 
   124→need to define the geographic limits of what we are calling the southwest; i.e.
   125→the easternmost, westernmost, southernmost, and northernmost points. 
   126→
   127→> Question: how would you find the latitude and longitude values to define this 
   128→extent?
   129→
   130→For this lesson, we can get away with using whole degree values; we will use 
   131→decimal degrees later in the lesson. When defining an area with four values 
   132→(easternmost, westernmost, etc.), we need to be sure to get them in the right 
   133→order. Often (but _not_ always), they are in the following order:
   134→
   135→1. xmin = westernmost
   136→2. xmax = easternmost
   137→3. ymin = southernmost
   138→4. ymax = northernmost
   139→
   140→```{r define-southwest}
   141→# Lower right: 21, -98
   142→# Upper left: 46, -126
   143→
   144→```
   145→
   146→You will also note that we used negative values for those western hemisphere 
   147→longitudes (-126, -98). The same goes for values in the southern hemisphere.
   148→
   149→> Question: Why would we use negative values instead of the letters W and S for 
   150→western longitudes and southern latitudes, respectively?
   151→
   152→We can do a reality check and print out the value by running the name of the 
   153→extent.
   154→
   155→```{r print-extent}
   156→
   157→```
   158→
   159→Finally, we can make a new map, with only those values in the area we defined 
   160→by our geographic extent with the `crop()` function from terra.
   161→
   162→```{r crop-temperature-data}
   163→
   164→```
   165→
   166→We can then compare the objects of our original raster:
   167→
   168→```{r uncropped-temp}
   169→
   170→```
   171→
   172→to the cropped raster:
   173→
   174→```{r cropped-temp}
   175→
   176→```
   177→
   178→Just about all the data changed, with the exception of the value in the 
   179→`coord. ref.` field. We will talk more about that field later.
   180→
   181→Let us look at the map of our cropped data, again with the `plot()` function.
   182→
   183→```{r plot-cropped-temp}
   184→
   185→```
   186→
   187→### Modifying data
   188→
   189→The _really_ nice thing about modern geospatial package in R is that we can do 
   190→operations on every cell of the data. For example maybe we want to convert our 
   191→temperature values from Celsius to Fahrenheit.
   192→
   193→> Question: For our cropped data, how many calculations would this conversion 
   194→require?
   195→
   196→A reminder of the formula for converting Celsius to Fahrenheit:
   197→
   198→$$
   199→F = (C \times \frac{9}{5}) + 32
   200→$$
   201→
   202→```{r temp-conversion}
   203→# (C * 9/5) + 32 = F
   204→temp_sw_f <- (temp_sw * 9/5) + 32
   205→```
   206→
   207→And plotting the data, the image looks the pretty much the same, but note the 
   208→scale is different:
   209→
   210→```{r plot-fahrenheit}
   211→
   212→```
   213→
   214→### Aside: The world is not flat, so why is my map?
   215→
   216→> Question: What are some differences between the map we see on our screen and 
   217→reality?
   218→
   219→The process of taking the three-dimensional shape of the Earth and presenting 
   220→it in a two-dimensional format (i.e. a map) is called projection and there are 
   221→several ways of doing this. And when I say several, I actually mean infinite. 
   222→From the [Wikipedia page on map projections](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_map_projections): 
   223→"Because there is no limit to the number of possible map projections, there can 
   224→be no comprehensive list." However, there are common ones that you should 
   225→probably stick to.
   226→
   227→When working in R, the coordinate reference system describes how the three-
   228→dimensional data are transformed into two-dimensional space.
   229→
   230→Let us look once more at our cropped temperature raster metadata.
   231→
   232→```{r show-wgs84}
   233→
   234→```
   235→
   236→Specifically, we see the value in the coord. ref. field is 
   237→
   238→`lon/lat WGS 84 (EPSG:4326)`
   239→
   240→Without going into too much detail, this tells us it uses the World Geodetic 
   241→System 84 reference system with latitude and longitude. This is a pretty good
   242→one to use as long as your maps aren't super close to the poles. _All_ 
   243→projections from three-dimensional space to two-dimensional space result in 
   244→some distortions, and this one really stretches out area near the poles. You 
   245→can see this in action by plotting your original map:
   246→
   247→```{r show-pole-distortion}
   248→
   249→```
   250→
   251→Look at Antarctica! It's huge!
   252→
   253→We can change the way our map looks by re-projecting the raster. That is, we 
   254→make a new object in R that uses a different coordinate reference system. You 
   255→might have noticed in the coord. ref. field that there was the extra bit of 
   256→text in parentheses: "(EPSG:4326)". Thankfully there is a set of unique 
   257→identifiers for all the different coordinate reference systems and we can use 
   258→those identifiers to re-project our data. These identifiers all start with the 
   259→letters "epsg" and end with a series of four to five numbers.
   260→
   261→As an example, we can re-project our southwest temperature data to a 
   262→projection that does not distort the area quite as much. We do this with the 
   263→terra function `project()` and then plot the result:
   264→
   265→```{r reproject-temp}
   266→
   267→
   268→```
   269→
   270→> Question: What is the name of the coordinate system that EPSG 5070 refers to?
   271→
   272→### Colors on maps
   273→
   274→So, we have a map, but these colors make me think of elevation, not 
   275→temperature, so how can we change this? Let's start by looking at the 
   276→documentation one of the functions for creating color palettes, called 
   277→`rainbow()`:
   278→
   279→```{r color-documentation, eval = FALSE}
   280→# Brings up R documentation for some color palettes
   281→
   282→```
   283→
   284→This actually brings up documentation for a bunch of palettes, but looking at 
   285→the entry in the **Usage** section for `rainbow()`, we see that we need to give
   286→it a value for the number of colors, `n`. Let us try passing the rainbow 
   287→palette to the plot function, giving it fifty colors:
   288→
   289→```{r rainbow-colors}
   290→
   291→```
   292→
   293→Well, the colors changed, but I would not say for the better. In fact, when I 
   294→go about making decisions about colors, my go-to resource is ColorBrewer at 
   295→[https://colorbrewer2.org](https://colorbrewer2.org).
   296→
   297→You can click through the options and it will display the different palettes. 
   298→For our purposes, the Yellow-Orange-Brown palette ("YlOrBr") should work well. 
   299→To see the name of the palette, look just to the left of the "EXPORT" tab:
   300→
   301→![Example of palette name in ColorBrewer, YlOrBr](colorbrewer-palette-name.png)
   302→
   303→To use these palettes, we replace the `rainbow()` function with the 
   304→`hcl.colors()` function, and indicate which palette we want through the 
   305→`palette` argument:
   306→
   307→```{r pick-colors}
   308→
   309→```
   310→
   311→This is close, but I'd rather reverse the scale, so darker colors indicate 
   312→hotter temperatures. I am going to create a variable that stores these colors
   313→so I do not have to keep writing out the `hcl.colors()` specification.
   314→
   315→```{r reverse-colors}
   316→
   317→
   318→```
   319→
   320→> Question: What are the values stored in the `temp_cols` variable?
   321→
   322→## Geospatial Vector Data
   323→
   324→Now we will switch to vector data, which are all based on coordinates. These 
   325→data commonly come as points (a single pair of coordinates), lines (multiple 
   326→pairs of coordinates), or polygons (multiple pairs of coordinates that define a 
   327→geographical area).
   328→
   329→### Adding Points
   330→
   331→Let us create a data frame with some city coordinates that we can add to the 
   332→map.
   333→
   334→```{r city-data-frame}
   335→
   336→```
   337→
   338→To make these data easier to work with our raster object, we are going to 
   339→transform it to a spatial vector, or SpatVector in terra-speak. While we are at 
   340→it, we will tell it to use the same coordinate reference system as the 
   341→temperature data we already loaded in.
   342→
   343→```{r cities-vector}
   344→
   345→```
   346→
   347→Looking at the SpatVector object, it doesn't really look very different.
   348→
   349→```{r print-cities-vector}
   350→
   351→```
   352→
   353→Now we can make our map and add those points to the map. Make sure to use 
   354→`add = TRUE` in our second call to `plot()` so it adds the points to the 
   355→already existing map.
   356→
   357→```{r add-cities-points}
   358→
   359→
   360→```
   361→
   362→Finally, because we stored the names of the cities in our data frame, we can 
   363→also add those.
   364→
   365→```{r add-cities-names}
   366→
   367→
   368→
   369→```
   370→
   371→### Adding Areas (aka Polygons)
   372→
   373→What if we want to add areas, not just points, to our map? Here is where we get 
   374→into the realm of polygons. We can do things like add polygons from shapefiles 
   375→that we download or export from a program like ArcGIS. For this lesson, though, 
   376→we are going to make a polygon on the fly and add that to our map.
   377→
   378→Going back to our original goal of a map highlighting desert biomes, we will 
   379→define a desert as an area that receives less than 250 millimeters of 
   380→precipitation per year (_Aside_: this definition only goes so far; for example, 
   381→the city of Tucson does not, technically, get counted as being a desert because 
   382→it receives an average of 300 mm of precipitation per year. You can read more 
   383→about [defining deserts on Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desert#Defining_characteristics)).
   384→
   385→So we start by loading in a file with global precipitation data:
   386→
   387→```{r read-precipitation-data}
   388→
   389→
   390→```
   391→
   392→Then cropping those data to the North American southwest (that we defined 
   393→above).
   394→
   395→```{r crop-precipitaton-data}
   396→
   397→
   398→```
   399→
   400→And it is usually good to do a reality check and plot the data, too:
   401→
   402→```{r plot-precipitation-data}
   403→
   404→```
   405→
   406→Cool. Map looks good and the ranges of precipitation seem reasonable, too. Now 
   407→we need to use this information to identify those areas considered deserts. We 
   408→will use this categorization to draw an outline of desert biomes on our 
   409→temperature map. As we talked about before, we will use the precipitation-based 
   410→definition of a desert, where any spot that received less than 250 mm in annual 
   411→precipitation is categorized as a desert. We start by defining our cutoff and 
   412→then creating a new raster based on values in the precipitation raster.
   413→
   414→```{r define-desert}
   415→
   416→
   417→
   418→```
   419→
   420→Looking at the raster metadata, we see the big difference is the min and max 
   421→values - they aren't numbers anymore, but are now logicals (`FALSE` and 
   422→`TRUE`). This is because what we stored in the `prec_desert` object was the 
   423→output of a comparison. That is, for each cell in our southwest precipitation 
   424→raster, `prec_sw`, we asked if the value in that cell was less than 250 mm. The 
   425→answer is either `TRUE` or `FALSE`, so that is what is stored in the resulting 
   426→output raster, `prec_desert`. We can also see this when we plot the output:
   427→
   428→```{r plot-desert-logical}
   429→
   430→```
   431→
   432→The areas that are lighting up in green are those "desert" biomes. For now, we 
   433→are going to ignore the limitations of our precipitation-based definition (i.e. 
   434→the southern portion of the Central Valley of California is not generally 
   435→considered a desert).
   436→
   437→To add this to our map, though, we do not want a logical raster, rather, we 
   438→want a polygon, in order to draw a border around the deserts. We start by 
   439→changing all the `FALSE` values to missing (`NA`) - if we did not do this, R 
   440→would make _two_ polygons from the data, one for desert areas and one for "not 
   441→desert" areas. We then use the `as.polygons()` function to do the 
   442→transformation and plot the polygon alone as a reality check.
   443→
   444→```{r desert-to-polygon}
   445→# Change all FALSE values to missing data
   446→
   447→# Convert remaining non-missing values to a polygon (vector data)
   448→
   449→# Plot polygons (reality check)
   450→
   451→```
   452→
   453→Looking good! Now we can plot our temperature map again and add the polygon to 
   454→the map. Feel free to experiment with arguments to change the colors and 
   455→transparency of the desert polygon (hint: look at the documentation for the 
   456→`polys()` function via `?polys` for details on how to do this).
   457→
   458→```{r add-desert}
   459→# Plot base temperature map
   460→
   461→# Add desert polygon
   462→
   463→```
   464→
   465→Finally, because we are being pedantic, let us make the plot again, this time 
   466→adding a title explaining what the colors mean ("00B0" is Unicode for the 
   467→degree symbol).
   468→
   469→```{r add-title}
   470→
   471→
   472→
   473→```
   474→
   475→And if you are inclined, you can output this map by wrapping all the plotting 
   476→commands within a call to one of the graphics device functions (e.g. `png()`, 
   477→`pdf()`) and then turning off this graphical redirect with `dev.off()`.
   478→
   479→```{r save-plot}
   480→
   481→plot(temp_sw, col = temp_cols, 
   482→     main = "Mean Annual Temperature (\u00B0C)")
   483→polys(x = desert_poly)
   484→
   485→```
   486→
   487→## Glossary
   488→
   489→These are the functions we used in this lesson; use the `?` in R to find more 
   490→details (e.g. `?rast`). The following are from the terra package:
   491→
   492→+ `as.polygons`: Convert a raster object into a polygon (shape) object.
   493→+ `crop`: Crop the geographic extent of a raster.
   494→+ `ext`: Create a SpatExtent (spatial extent) object.
   495→+ `plot`: Um, plot?
   496→+ `polys`: Add polygons to an existing plot.
   497→+ `project`: Change the projection of a geospatial object.
   498→+ `rast`: Read in a raster file (`rast()` does more than this, too, but this is 
   499→what we used it for here).
   500→+ `text`: Add text to an existing plot.
   501→
   502→And we used a few from base R, too:
   503→
   504→+ `dev.off`: Stop writing to a graphics output file.
   505→+ `hcl.colors`: Create a vector of color values based on a palette.
   506→+ `png`: Start writing to a PNG graphics file.
   507→+ `rainbow`: Create a vector of unicorn-vomit colors.
   508→+ `rev`: Reverse the order of values in a vector.
   509→
   510→### Other Resources
   511→
   512→+ A really nice [guide to using terra in R](https://rspatial.org/).
   513→+ A cheat-sheet of sorts for 
   514→[terra functions](https://rspatial.github.io/terra/reference/terra-package.html).
   515→+ The [sf package](https://r-spatial.github.io/sf/) is another way to work with 
   516→geospatial data in R. It offers a lot of functionality and works well (most of 
   517→the time) with terra objects.
   518→+ The [OpenStreetMap](https://www.openstreetmap.org) (OSM) project also has an 
   519→R package, [osmdata](https://docs.ropensci.org/osmdata/), that allows you to 
   520→download and use data available from OSM.
   521→+ To really impress your friends, the [tidyterra](https://dieghernan.github.io/tidyterra/)
   522→package offers functions for using terra objects (SpatRasters, SpatVectors, 
   523→etc.) with the ggplot2 package.
   524→+ For a more in-depth look at geospatial applications in R, check out the Data 
   525→Carpentry lesson introducing 
   526→[raster & vector data in R](https://datacarpentry.org/r-raster-vector-geospatial/).
   527→
